content.problemStatement:
Alice likes numbers which are even, and are a multiple of 7.
Bob likes numbers which are odd, and are a multiple of 9.
Alice, Bob, and Charlie find a number AAA.


If Alice likes AAA, Alice takes home the number.
If Bob likes AAA, Bob takes home the number.
If both Alice and Bob don't like the number, Charlie takes it home.
Given AAA, find who takes it home.

**Note:** You can prove that there is no integer AAA such that both Alice and Bob like it.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of a single integer, AAA.


Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line who takes the number home - "Alice", "Bob", or "Charlie".


You may print each character in uppercase or lowercase. For example, `Alice`, `alice`, `aLiCe`, and `ALICE` are all considered identical.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a single integer A.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output on a separate line the name of the person who takes the number home: "Alice", "Bob" or "Charlie".

sampleTestCases.explanation: For each number we apply the rules: 7 is odd and not a multiple of 9 → Charlie; 14 is even and a multiple of 7 → Alice; 21 is odd not multiple of 9 → Charlie; 18 is even not multiple of 7 → Charlie; 27 is odd multiple of 9 → Bob; 63 is odd multiple of 9 → Bob; 126 is even multiple of 7 → Alice; 8 is even not multiple of 7 → Charlie.
9 is odd and divisible by 9 → Bob. 28 is even and divisible by 7 → Alice. 45 is odd but not divisible by 9 → Charlie.
1: odd not multiple of 9 → Charlie. 2: even not multiple of 7 → Charlie. 63: odd multiple of 9 → Bob. 126: even multiple of 7 → Alice.

solution.approach: For each value A we check the two liking conditions. Alice likes A if it is even (A%2==0) and a multiple of 7 (A%7==0). Bob likes A if it is odd (A%2==1) and a multiple of 9 (A%9==0). The statement guarantees that both conditions can never be true simultaneously, so we can test Alice first, then Bob, otherwise Charlie gets the number. This is O(1) per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) – constant work for each of the T test cases.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few variables are used regardless of input size.
